{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

[[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taikō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taikō]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:太閤]]

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.

During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taikō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taikō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taikō]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:太閤]]

{{Otheruses|称号としての太閤|[[名古屋市]][[中村区]]太閤|太閤 (名古屋市)}} '''太閤'''（たいこう）は、[[摂政]]または[[関白]]の職を退いて後、子が摂関の職に就いた者{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}、摂関辞職後に[[内覧]]の[[宣旨]]を受けたものを指す称号<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=太閤|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=太閤|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>。敬称は摂政・関白と同じく「[[殿下]]」であり、呼びかけの場合は「太閤殿下」となる。本来は'''太閤下'''（たいこうか）と呼ばれていたが{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}、やがて略されるようになった。'''大殿'''（おおとの）とも呼ばれる{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}。
また[[出家]]した太閤のことを'''禅定太閤'''（ぜんじょう たいこう）、略して'''禅閤'''（ぜんこう）という{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}。
== 沿革 == 古くは摂関・[[太政大臣]]現職者を指す尊称であった<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>。『[[西宮記]]』や『[[左経記]]』には在職中の摂関が太閤を称した記述があり、「関白太閤」と称されていた{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=49}}。
摂政・関白を[[嫡男]]に譲った者が「太閤」と呼ばれる例は[[平安時代]]からあった。例えば[[藤原道長]]は[[後一条天皇]]の摂政を息子[[藤原頼通|頼通]]に譲り、続いて任じられた[[太政大臣]]の職も辞任した後は「太閤」と呼ばれていた<ref>[[藤原実資]]『[[小右記]]』[[寛仁]]2年[[6月20日 (旧暦)|6月20日]]条</ref>。[[鎌倉時代]]の関白、[[二条良実]]は弟の[[一条実経]]に関白の地位を譲った後に引き続き[[内覧]]に任じられたことから「太閤」の名乗りを用いた<ref>『[[五代帝王物語]]』</ref>。同様に[[室町時代]]の[[二条良基]]は13年にわたって関白を務めて辞職した直後に内覧宣旨を受け、太閤を号した<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=小川剛生 |date=1995-12 |url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001 |title=二条良基の除目説（下） : 摂関家説の伝授をめぐって |journal=三田國文 |ISSN=0287-9204 |publisher=慶應義塾大学国文学研究室 |volume=23 |pages=1-9 |doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001 |naid=110000376031 |CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>。
=== 豊太閤と江戸時代の太閤 === [[豊臣秀吉]]が[[養子縁組|養嗣子]]の[[豊臣秀次|秀次]]に関白を譲った後も慣例より太閤と呼ばれたが、秀吉の死後も太閤といえば秀吉を指すことが多く、[[太閤検地]]のような語を構成することもある。このことから「[[大師 (僧)|大師]]は[[空海|弘法]]に奪われ、太閤は秀吉に奪わる」という[[格言]]までできるほどだった。特に秀吉を指すことを強調する場合は'''豊太閤'''（ほうたいこう）と呼ばれる。
その後の江戸時代も、摂関を子の[[一条道香]]に譲った[[一条兼香]]は太閤と呼ばれた。[[安政]]3年12月9日（1857年1月4日）に、関白辞任後（後任は別家の[[九条尚忠]]）も内覧に留まった[[鷹司政通]]が[[孝明天皇]]の勅旨をもって太閤の称号を贈られたのが太閤の最後の例となる。
=== 今太閤 === [[明治維新]]で旧来の太政官制が廃止され摂政・関白がなくなると、太閤の語もやがて過去のものとなるはずだったが、百姓の身分から初代[[内閣総理大臣]]に上り詰め、その後も[[元老]]として明治日本を牽引し、艶福家の点でも共通する[[伊藤博文]]が、豊太閤に倣って'''今太閤'''（いまたいこう）と呼ばれるようになった。さらに[[戦後#日本での「戦後」の位置づけ|戦後]]には[[高等教育]]の学歴を持たずに内閣総理大臣まで上り詰めた[[田中角栄]]も今太閤と呼ばれるようになった。
このように「今太閤」は貧しい生まれから立身出世して大きな権力を握るに至った者の代名詞として使われるようになり、この他にも総理大臣の[[三木武吉]]、[[阪急電鉄]]の[[小林一三]]、[[パナソニック|松下電器]]の[[松下幸之助]]、[[大映]]の[[永田雅一]]らも今太閤と、また[[吉本興業]]の[[吉本せい]]は女今太閤と呼ばれた。
== 太閤記 == 豊臣秀吉の一代記の作品群は『[[太閤記]]』と呼ばれている<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=金時徳 |date=2009-02 |url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730 |title=国会図書館蔵『絵本武勇大功記』の翻刻と解題 |journal=国文学研究資料館紀要 |ISSN=0387-3447 |publisher=国文学研究資料館 |issue=35 |pages=209-240 |doi=10.24619/00000727 |CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>。ただし、[[浄瑠璃]]や[[歌舞伎]]など演劇では「太閤」の官職名を「大功」に代えて『大功記』のタイトルにしている作品がある<ref name="ehon" />。

{{DEFAULTSORT:たいこう}} [[Category:摂関|*たいこう]] [[Category:古代日本の称号]] [[Category:中世日本の称号]] [[Category:近世日本の称号]] [[Category:領主・貴族の称号]] [[Category:豊臣秀吉]]

{{Otheruses|称号としての太閤|[[名古屋市]][[中村区]]太閤|太閤 (名古屋市)}} '''太閤'''（たいこう）は、[[摂政]]または[[関白]]の職を退いて後、子が摂関の職に就いた者{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}、摂関辞職後に[[内覧]]の[[宣旨]]を受けたものを指す称号<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=太閤|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=太閤|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>。敬称は摂政・関白と同じく「[[殿下]]」であり、呼びかけの場合は「太閤殿下」となる。本来は'''太閤下'''（たいこうか）と呼ばれていたが{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}、やがて略されるようになった。'''大殿'''（おおとの）とも呼ばれる{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}。
また[[出家]]した太閤のことを'''禅定太閤'''（ぜんじょう たいこう）、略して'''禅閤'''（ぜんこう）という{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=48}}。
== 沿革 == 古くは摂関・[[太政大臣]]現職者を指す尊称であった<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>。『[[西宮記]]』や『[[左経記]]』には在職中の摂関が太閤を称した記述があり、「関白太閤」と称されていた{{sfn|和田英松|1983|p=49}}。
摂政・関白を[[嫡男]]に譲った者が「太閤」と呼ばれる例は[[平安時代]]からあった。例えば[[藤原道長]]は[[後一条天皇]]の摂政を息子[[藤原頼通|頼通]]に譲り、続いて任じられた[[太政大臣]]の職も辞任した後は「太閤」と呼ばれていた<ref>[[藤原実資]]『[[小右記]]』[[寛仁]]2年[[6月20日 (旧暦)|6月20日]]条</ref>。[[鎌倉時代]]の関白、[[二条良実]]は弟の[[一条実経]]に関白の地位を譲った後に引き続き[[内覧]]に任じられたことから「太閤」の名乗りを用いた<ref>『[[五代帝王物語]]』</ref>。同様に[[室町時代]]の[[二条良基]]は13年にわたって関白を務めて辞職した直後に内覧宣旨を受け、太閤を号した<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=小川剛生 |date=1995-12 |url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001 |title=二条良基の除目説（下） : 摂関家説の伝授をめぐって |journal=三田國文 |ISSN=0287-9204 |publisher=慶應義塾大学国文学研究室 |volume=23 |pages=1-9 |doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001 |naid=110000376031 |CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>。
=== 豊太閤と江戸時代の太閤 === [[豊臣秀吉]]が[[養子縁組|養嗣子]]の[[豊臣秀次|秀次]]に関白を譲った後も慣例より太閤と呼ばれたが、秀吉の死後も太閤といえば秀吉を指すことが多く、[[太閤検地]]のような語を構成することもある。このことから「[[大師 (僧)|大師]]は[[空海|弘法]]に奪われ、太閤は秀吉に奪わる」という[[格言]]までできるほどだった。特に秀吉を指すことを強調する場合は'''豊太閤'''（ほうたいこう）と呼ばれる。
その後の江戸時代も、摂関を子の[[一条道香]]に譲った[[一条兼香]]は太閤と呼ばれた。[[安政]]3年12月9日（1857年1月4日）に、関白辞任後（後任は別家の[[九条尚忠]]）も内覧に留まった[[鷹司政通]]が[[孝明天皇]]の勅旨をもって太閤の称号を贈られたのが太閤の最後の例となる。
=== 今太閤 === [[明治維新]]で旧来の太政官制が廃止され摂政・関白がなくなると、太閤の語もやがて過去のものとなるはずだったが、百姓の身分から初代[[内閣総理大臣]]に上り詰め、その後も[[元老]]として明治日本を牽引し、艶福家の点でも共通する[[伊藤博文]]が、豊太閤に倣って'''今太閤'''（いまたいこう）と呼ばれるようになった。さらに[[戦後#日本での「戦後」の位置づけ|戦後]]には[[高等教育]]の学歴を持たずに内閣総理大臣まで上り詰めた[[田中角栄]]も今太閤と呼ばれるようになった。
このように「今太閤」は貧しい生まれから立身出世して大きな権力を握るに至った者の代名詞として使われるようになり、この他にも戦前戦後の政治家[[三木武吉]]（[[三木武夫]]とは血縁関係無）、[[阪急電鉄]]の[[小林一三]]、[[パナソニック|松下電器]]の[[松下幸之助]]、[[大映]]の[[永田雅一]]らも今太閤と、また[[吉本興業]]の[[吉本せい]]は女今太閤と呼ばれた。
== 太閤記 == 豊臣秀吉の一代記の作品群は『[[太閤記]]』と呼ばれている<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=金時徳 |date=2009-02 |url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730 |title=国会図書館蔵『絵本武勇大功記』の翻刻と解題 |journal=国文学研究資料館紀要 |ISSN=0387-3447 |publisher=国文学研究資料館 |issue=35 |pages=209-240 |doi=10.24619/00000727 |CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>。ただし、[[浄瑠璃]]や[[歌舞伎]]など演劇では「太閤」の官職名を「大功」に代えて『大功記』のタイトルにしている作品がある<ref name="ehon" />。

{{DEFAULTSORT:たいこう}} [[Category:摂関|*たいこう]] [[Category:古代日本の称号]] [[Category:中世日本の称号]] [[Category:近世日本の称号]] [[Category:領主・貴族の称号]] [[Category:豊臣秀吉]]

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所}}

The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所}}
{{Toyotomi administration}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区|12=draft}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政|12=draft}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=draft}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧|12=draft}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=draft}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長|12=draft}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇|12=draft}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=draft}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁|12=draft}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日|12=draft}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実|12=draft}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経|12=draft}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=draft}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基|12=draft}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次|12=draft}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通|12=draft}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|12=draft}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄|12=draft}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉|12=draft}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助|12=draft}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器|12=draft}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=draft}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記|12=draft}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|12=draft}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=draft}}
{{Toyotomi administration}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区|12=draft}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政|12=draft}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=draft}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧|12=draft}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=draft}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長|12=draft}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇|12=draft}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=draft}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁|12=draft}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日|12=draft}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実|12=draft}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経|12=draft}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=draft}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基|12=draft}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次|12=draft}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通|12=draft}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|12=draft}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄|12=draft}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉|12=draft}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助|12=draft}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器|12=draft}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=draft}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記|12=draft}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|12=draft}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=draft}}

The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō Daijin|ja|太政大臣|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=draft}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|ja|藤原道長|12=draft}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|ja|後一条天皇|12=draft}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|ja|藤原実資|12=draft}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=draft}}'' {{ill|Kannin|ja|寛仁|12=draft}} 2, {{ill|June 20 (old calendar)|ja|6月20日|12=draft}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimitsu|ja|二条良実|12=draft}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|ja|一条実経|12=draft}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=draft}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|ja|二条良基|12=draft}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|ja|豊臣秀次|12=draft}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|ja|鷹司政通|12=draft}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文|12=draft}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Tanaka Kakuei|ja|田中角栄|12=draft}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Miki Bukichi|ja|三木武吉|12=draft}}, industrialists like {{ill|Kobayashi Ichizō|ja|小林一三|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hankyu Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Matsushita Kōnosuke|ja|松下幸之助|12=draft}} of {{ill|Panasonic|ja|松下電器|12=draft}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=draft}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|ja|太閤記|12=draft}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri|ja|浄瑠璃|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kabuki|ja|歌舞伎|12=draft}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|ja|中村区|12=draft}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Sesshō|ja|摂政|12=draft}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=draft}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|ja|内覧|12=draft}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
