{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367105]]
Wikidata name: taikō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in [[:en:Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya]]}}
'''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former [[:en:Regent|Sesshō]] or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of [[:en:Nairan|Nairan]]<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.

A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.

== Historical Development ==

=== Early Usage ===
The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]]<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.

The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Michinaga|Fujiwara no Michinaga]] was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for [[:en:Emperor Go-Ichijō|Emperor Go-Ichijō]] and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>[[:en:Fujiwara no Sanesuke|Fujiwara no Sanesuke]], ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' [[:en:Kannin|Kannin]] 2, [[:en:June 20|June 20 (old calendar)]] entry</ref>. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshizane|Nijō Yoshimitsu]] adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, [[:en:Ichijō Sanetsune|Ichijō Sanetsune]], and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshimoto|Nijō Yoshimoto]] held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.

=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō ===
[[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]]. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who received the designation in [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]].

=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" ===
With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]], Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, [[:en:Kakuei Tanaka|Tanaka Kakuei]], who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."

The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like [[:en:Bukichi Miki|Miki Bukichi]], industrialists like [[:en:Ichizō Kobayashi|Kobayashi Ichizō]] of [[:en:Hankyu|Hankyu Railway]], and entrepreneurs like [[:en:Kōnosuke Matsushita|Matsushita Kōnosuke]] of [[:en:Panasonic|Panasonic]]. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."

== Taikō Chronicles ==
Biographies of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] are collectively referred to as the ''[[:en:Taikōki|Taikōki]]''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]] and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]]<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Michinaga|Fujiwara no Michinaga]] was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for [[:en:Emperor Go-Ichijō|Emperor Go-Ichijō]] and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>[[:en:Fujiwara no Sanesuke|Fujiwara no Sanesuke]], ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' [[:en:Kannin|Kannin]] 2, [[:en:June 20|June 20 (old calendar)]] entry</ref>. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshizane|Nijō Yoshimitsu]] adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, [[:en:Ichijō Sanetsune|Ichijō Sanetsune]], and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshimoto|Nijō Yoshimoto]] held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]]. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who received the designation in [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]].
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]], Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, [[:en:Kakuei Tanaka|Tanaka Kakuei]], who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like [[:en:Bukichi Miki|Miki Bukichi]], industrialists like [[:en:Ichizō Kobayashi|Kobayashi Ichizō]] of [[:en:Hankyu|Hankyu Railway]], and entrepreneurs like [[:en:Kōnosuke Matsushita|Matsushita Kōnosuke]] of [[:en:Panasonic|Panasonic]]. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] are collectively referred to as the ''[[:en:Taikōki|Taikōki]]''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]] and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367105]] Wikidata name: taikō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in [[:en:Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya]]}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former [[:en:Regent|Sesshō]] or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of [[:en:Nairan|Nairan]]<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]]<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Michinaga|Fujiwara no Michinaga]] was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for [[:en:Emperor Go-Ichijō|Emperor Go-Ichijō]] and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>[[:en:Fujiwara no Sanesuke|Fujiwara no Sanesuke]], ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' [[:en:Kannin|Kannin]] 2, [[:en:June 20|June 20 (old calendar)]] entry</ref>. This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who received the designation in [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]].

However, figures like [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]], Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, [[:en:Kakuei Tanaka|Tanaka Kakuei]], who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like [[:en:Bukichi Miki|Miki Bukichi]], industrialists like [[:en:Ichizō Kobayashi|Kobayashi Ichizō]] of [[:en:Hankyu|Hankyu Railway]], and entrepreneurs like [[:en:Kōnosuke Matsushita|Matsushita Kōnosuke]] of [[:en:Panasonic|Panasonic]]. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."

[[ja:太閤]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]] [[ko:태합]] [[zh:太閤]] {{wikidata link|Q5367105}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Sesshō and Kampaku]] [[Category:Titles of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Titles of Japan in the Early Modern period]] [[Category:Titles of Medieval Japan]] [[Category:Titles of nobility]] [[Category:Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367105]] Wikidata name: taikō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in [[:en:Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya]]}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former [[:en:Regent|Sesshō]] or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of [[:en:Nairan|Nairan]]<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and [[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō Daijin]]<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Michinaga|Fujiwara no Michinaga]] was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for [[:en:Emperor Go-Ichijō|Emperor Go-Ichijō]] and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>[[:en:Fujiwara no Sanesuke|Fujiwara no Sanesuke]], ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' [[:en:Kannin|Kannin]] 2, [[:en:June 20|June 20 (old calendar)]] entry</ref>. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshizane|Nijō Yoshimitsu]] adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, [[:en:Ichijō Sanetsune|Ichijō Sanetsune]], and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshimoto|Nijō Yoshimoto]] held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]]. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who received the designation in [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]].
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]], Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, [[:en:Kakuei Tanaka|Tanaka Kakuei]], who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like [[:en:Bukichi Miki|Miki Bukichi]], industrialists like [[:en:Ichizō Kobayashi|Kobayashi Ichizō]] of [[:en:Hankyu|Hankyu Railway]], and entrepreneurs like [[:en:Kōnosuke Matsushita|Matsushita Kōnosuke]] of [[:en:Panasonic|Panasonic]]. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] are collectively referred to as the ''[[:en:Taikōki|Taikōki]]''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]] and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshizane|Nijō Yoshimitsu]] adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, [[:en:Ichijō Sanetsune|Ichijō Sanetsune]], and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Nijō Yoshimoto|Nijō Yoshimoto]] held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, [[:en:Toyotomi Hidetsugu|Toyotomi Hidetsugu]]. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who received the designation in [[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Kōmei]].
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like [[:en:Itō Hirobumi|Itō Hirobumi]], Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, [[:en:Kakuei Tanaka|Tanaka Kakuei]], who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like [[:en:Bukichi Miki|Miki Bukichi]], industrialists like [[:en:Ichizō Kobayashi|Kobayashi Ichizō]] of [[:en:Hankyu|Hankyu Railway]], and entrepreneurs like [[:en:Kōnosuke Matsushita|Matsushita Kōnosuke]] of [[:en:Panasonic|Panasonic]]. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] are collectively referred to as the ''[[:en:Taikōki|Taikōki]]''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as [[:en:Jōruri (music)|Jōruri]] and [[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]], the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367105]] Wikidata name: taikō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q780521|lt=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|WD=Q477406|lt=Sesshō}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|en|Nairan|WD=Q5365623|lt=Nairan}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|WD=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|WD=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|WD=Q1193951|lt=Kannin}} 2, {{ill|June 20|en|June 20|WD=Q2666|lt=June 20 (old calendar)}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshizane|en|Nijō Yoshizane|WD=Q3341492|lt=Nijō Yoshimitsu}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|en|Ichijō Sanetsune|WD=Q370351|lt=Ichijō Sanetsune}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Kakuei Tanaka|en|Kakuei Tanaka|WD=Q297644|lt=Tanaka Kakuei}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]] [[ko:태합]] [[zh:太閤]] {{wikidata link|Q5367105}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Sesshō and Kampaku]] [[Category:Titles of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Titles of Japan in the Early Modern period]] [[Category:Titles of Medieval Japan]] [[Category:Titles of nobility]] [[Category:Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367105]] Wikidata name: taikō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q780521|lt=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|WD=Q477406|lt=Sesshō}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|en|Nairan|WD=Q5365623|lt=Nairan}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|WD=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|WD=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|WD=Q1193951|lt=Kannin}} 2, {{ill|June 20|en|June 20|WD=Q2666|lt=June 20 (old calendar)}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshizane|en|Nijō Yoshizane|WD=Q3341492|lt=Nijō Yoshimitsu}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|en|Ichijō Sanetsune|WD=Q370351|lt=Ichijō Sanetsune}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Kakuei Tanaka|en|Kakuei Tanaka|WD=Q297644|lt=Tanaka Kakuei}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q780521|lt=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|WD=Q477406|lt=Sesshō}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|en|Nairan|WD=Q5365623|lt=Nairan}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|WD=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|WD=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|WD=Q1193951|lt=Kannin}} 2, {{ill|June 20|en|June 20|WD=Q2666|lt=June 20 (old calendar)}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshizane|en|Nijō Yoshizane|WD=Q3341492|lt=Nijō Yoshimitsu}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|en|Ichijō Sanetsune|WD=Q370351|lt=Ichijō Sanetsune}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Kakuei Tanaka|en|Kakuei Tanaka|WD=Q297644|lt=Tanaka Kakuei}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]]
{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q780521|lt=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|WD=Q477406|lt=Sesshō}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|en|Nairan|WD=Q5365623|lt=Nairan}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|WD=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|WD=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|WD=Q1193951|lt=Kannin}} 2, {{ill|June 20|en|June 20|WD=Q2666|lt=June 20 (old calendar)}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshizane|en|Nijō Yoshizane|WD=Q3341492|lt=Nijō Yoshimitsu}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|en|Ichijō Sanetsune|WD=Q370351|lt=Ichijō Sanetsune}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Kakuei Tanaka|en|Kakuei Tanaka|WD=Q297644|lt=Tanaka Kakuei}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Taikō Shogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto|1=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.

The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi|1=Bukichi Miki}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō|1=Ichizō Kobayashi}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway|1=Hankyu}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke|1=Kōnosuke Matsushita}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic|1=Panasonic}}. == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|authorlink =和田英松 |author2 =Tokoro Isao (Ed.)|authorlink2 = 所功|title=Shintei Kanjō Yōkai (Revised Edition)|publisher = Kodansha|series =Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1983|origyear=1902|isbn= 4-06-158621-1|ref=harv}} * {{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}} == See Also == * {{ill|Taikō Shogi|ja|太閤将棋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō Shogi|1=Q11444538|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444538}} * {{ill|Ōtono|ja|大殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtono|1=ōtono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11436760}} * {{ill|Ōgosho|ja|大御所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgosho|1=ōgosho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5366647}}

A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==
=== Early Usage === The title initially served as an honorific for sitting Sesshō, Kampaku, and {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}<ref name="#1"/><ref name="#2"/>. Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
The practice of referring to retired regents as Taikō began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|WD=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was called Taikō after resigning from the position of regent for {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|WD=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō|1=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} and subsequently from the office of Daijō Daijin<ref>{{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|WD=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke|1=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|小右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}'' {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|WD=Q1193951|lt=Kannin|1=Kannin}} 2, {{ill|June 20|en|June 20|WD=Q2666|lt=June 20 (old calendar)|1=June 20}} entry</ref>. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshizane|en|Nijō Yoshizane|WD=Q3341492|lt=Nijō Yoshimitsu|1=Nijō Yoshizane}} adopted the title after passing the regency to his brother, {{ill|Ichijō Sanetsune|en|Ichijō Sanetsune|WD=Q370351|lt=Ichijō Sanetsune|1=Ichijō Sanetsune}}, and receiving the Nairan appointment<ref>{{ill|Godaiteiwamonogatari|ja|五代帝王物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godaiteiwamonogatari|1=Godai Teiō Monogatari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372013}}</ref>. Similarly, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto|1=Nijō Yoshimoto}} held the title Taikō after a lengthy tenure as Kampaku and receiving the Nairan appointment upon stepping down<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Takeshō Ogawa|date=1995-12|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN00296083-19951200-0001|title=Nijō Yoshimoto’s Appointments: A Study|journal=Mita Kokubun|ISSN=0287-9204|publisher=Keio University|volume=23|pages=1–9|doi=10.14991/002.19951200-0001|naid=110000376031|CRID=1390290699880614784}}</ref>.
=== Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Later Taikō === {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} was referred to as Taikō after transferring the Kampaku position to his adopted heir, {{ill|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|en|Toyotomi Hidetsugu|WD=Q967501|lt=Toyotomi Hidetsugu|1=Toyotomi Hidetsugu}}. Even after Hideyoshi's death, the term Taikō became synonymous with him, leading to usages like ''Taikō Kenchi'' (Taikō's Land Survey). This association became so strong that it led to the saying, "''Daishi'' belongs to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}, and Taikō belongs to Hideyoshi."
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, other regents who stepped down were also referred to as Taikō. The last individual formally granted the title was {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi|1=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who received the designation in {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei|1=Ansei}} 3 (1857) upon resigning as Kampaku but remaining as Nairan by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}.
=== Modern Usage: "Now Taikō" === With the abolition of the old {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官 (明治時代)}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the title Taikō ceased to exist in its traditional sense. However, figures like {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|en|Itō Hirobumi|WD=Q174971|lt=Itō Hirobumi|1=Itō Hirobumi}}, Japan's first Prime Minister, were referred to as "Now Taikō" (今太閤) due to their rise from humble beginnings to prominence, mirroring Hideyoshi. Later, {{ill|Kakuei Tanaka|en|Kakuei Tanaka|WD=Q297644|lt=Tanaka Kakuei|1=Kakuei Tanaka}}, who became Prime Minister without a university degree, was similarly dubbed "Now Taikō."
The term has since been used more broadly to describe individuals who achieved great power or influence from modest origins. Examples include politicians like {{ill|Bukichi Miki|en|Bukichi Miki|WD=Q24876|lt=Miki Bukichi|1=Bukichi Miki}}, industrialists like {{ill|Ichizō Kobayashi|en|Ichizō Kobayashi|WD=Q5364081|lt=Kobayashi Ichizō|1=Ichizō Kobayashi}} of {{ill|Hankyu|en|Hankyu|WD=Q473058|lt=Hankyu Railway|1=Hankyu}}, and entrepreneurs like {{ill|Kōnosuke Matsushita|en|Kōnosuke Matsushita|WD=Q252785|lt=Matsushita Kōnosuke|1=Kōnosuke Matsushita}} of {{ill|Panasonic|en|Panasonic|WD=Q53247|lt=Panasonic|1=Panasonic}}. Even entertainers such as {{ill|Yoshimoto Sei|ja|吉本せい|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimoto Sei|1=Sei Yoshimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413162}} earned the title, being referred to as "Female Now Taikō."
== Taikō Chronicles == Biographies of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} are collectively referred to as the ''{{ill|Taikōki|en|Taikōki|WD=Q1083343|lt=Taikōki|1=Taikōki}}''<ref name="ehon">{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kim Jitoku|date=2009-02|url=https://kokubunken.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/730|title=A Commentary on "Ehon Buyū Daikōki"|journal=National Institute of Japanese Literature Bulletin|ISSN=0387-3447|issue=35|pages=209–240|doi=10.24619/00000727|CRID=1390853649821347840}}</ref>. In theater, such as {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|WD=Q1361891|lt=Jōruri|1=Jōruri (music)}} and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, the term Taikō is often replaced with ''Daikō'' (大功) in titles like ''Daikōki''<ref name="ehon" />.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تایکو (عنوان)]][[ja:太閤]][[ko:태합]][[zh:太閤]]
{{Otheruses|Taikō as a title|{{ill|Taikō (Nagoya)|ja|太閤 (名古屋市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taikō (Nagoya)|1=Taikō, Nagoya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42848979}} in {{ill|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q780521|lt=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakamura-ku, Nagoya}}}} '''Taikō''' (太閤) is a title in Japan referring to a former {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|WD=Q477406|lt=Sesshō|1=Regent}} or {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|1=kampaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1199966}} who stepped down after transferring the position to their child{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}} or someone who, after resigning from the regency, received the imperial {{ill|Senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senji|qq=}} for the title of {{ill|Nairan|en|Nairan|WD=Q5365623|lt=Nairan|1=Nairan}}<ref name="#1">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=デジタル大辞泉}}</ref><ref name="#2">{{kotobank|1=Taikō|2=日本大百科全書}}</ref>. The honorific for Taikō, like for Sesshō and Kampaku, is "Denka" (殿下), and they are addressed as "Taikō Denka." Historically referred to as '''Taikōka''' (太閤下){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}, the term was later abbreviated. They are also called '''Ōtono''' (大殿){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
A Taikō who became a monk is called '''Zenjō Taikō''' (禅定太閤) or simply '''Zenkō''' (禅閤){{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=48}}.
== Historical Development ==

Records such as the {{ill|Nishinomiya-ki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya-ki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627667}} and the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} indicate that active regents were sometimes referred to as Taikō, with phrases like "Kampaku Taikō" appearing{{sfn|Hidematsu Wada|1983|p=49}}.
